extra.message: User input from POST /students/ (data['name']) flows to Student.create() which uses Python string formatting (% operator) to interpolate the name directly into the SQL query: `"VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}`. This is passed to cur.execute(q) without parameterization, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL (e.g., name="'); DROP TABLE students; --").

Jinja2 template engine is configured with autoescape=False, disabling automatic HTML escaping for all templates. This makes every template that renders user-controlled data vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting. User-controlled data such as student names, course titles/descriptions, and review text are rendered in multiple templates without the |e filter, allowing injection of arbitrary JavaScript.

User-controlled review text (submitted via POST /courses/{id}/review) is rendered as {{ review.review_text }} without HTML escaping. With autoescape=False in the Jinja2 configuration, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the review_text field, which will execute in the browser of any user viewing the course page.

User-controlled course title and description (submitted via POST /courses/) are rendered as {{ course.title }} and {{ course.description }} without HTML escaping. With autoescape=False, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via these fields.

User-controlled student name (submitted via POST /students/) is rendered as {{ name }} without HTML escaping in the students list. With autoescape=False, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the student name field.

User-controlled course title and description are rendered as {{ course.title }} and {{ course.description }} without HTML escaping in the courses listing page. With autoescape=False, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via these fields.

User-controlled course title is rendered as {{ course.title }} without HTML escaping in the review form page. With autoescape=False, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the course title field.

The CSRF middleware (csrf_middleware) is commented out in the application middleware list. All POST endpoints are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery: creating students, creating courses, submitting reviews, evaluating students, and logging out. An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits forms to these endpoints on behalf of an authenticated user.

Password verification uses unsalted MD5 hashing: `self.pwd_hash == md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()`. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing. No salt is used, making all password hashes vulnerable to rainbow table attacks and fast brute-force cracking. Should use a dedicated password hashing function like bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2.

cwe: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

extra.message: The POST handler for /students/ (creating new students) has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can create students by sending a POST request directly, bypassing the template's conditional form display. The @authorize() decorator is only used on the logout endpoint.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The POST handler for /courses/ (creating new courses) has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can create courses by sending a POST request directly. The template conditionally shows the form only for admin users, but the route itself enforces no such restriction.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The POST handler for /courses/{course_id}/review (creating course reviews) has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can submit reviews by sending a POST request directly.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The evaluate endpoint (POST /students/{student_id}/evaluate/{course_id}) has no authorization check. The template restricts the evaluate form display to admin users (auth_user.is_admin), but the route handler has no @authorize(ensure_admin=True) decorator. Any user (or unauthenticated attacker) can grade students by sending a POST request directly, bypassing the admin-only template restriction.

The 50x error template renders the full exception object details via {{ error.__dict__ }}, which can expose sensitive internal information such as file paths, database connection strings, stack traces, and application internals to end users. Combined with autoescape=False, this also constitutes an XSS vector if any error attribute contains user-controlled data.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The session cookie is created with httponly=False in the RedisStorage configuration. This makes the session cookie accessible to JavaScript (e.g., via document.cookie), allowing an attacker who achieves XSS to steal session identifiers and hijack user sessions.

Database credentials (user: postgres, password: postgres) are hardcoded in the configuration file. If this file is committed to version control, these credentials are exposed to anyone with repository access.

The aiohttp Application is created with debug=True. In production, debug mode can expose detailed error information, stack traces, and application internals to attackers, facilitating further exploitation.
